metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default timeout value for the MCP startup `run_ensures` lock budget (e.g., `timeout=2.0` seconds) so the executor has no discretionary choice to make.
findings.finding: The MCP startup lock timeout is described only as 'a few seconds' — an executor must pick a concrete value with no stated default, making the implementation under-specified.
findings.location: MCP server startup section: 'with a SHORT lock budget (a few seconds)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the MCP server startup section: 'Use `timeout=2.0, attempts=1` as the default; adjust only if benchmarking shows startup regression.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the MCP startup test description that case (a) must set up an existing tracker directory fixture so the `if it is an existing dir` guard is satisfied and `run_ensures` is actually invoked (not vacuously skipped).
findings.finding: The MCP startup test for case (a) — 'asserts `run_ensures` was called BEFORE `build_server().run()`' — does not specify that the tracker fixture is set up as an existing directory, yet the plan gates the `run_ensures` call on `if it is an existing dir`. If the test fixture omits this, the guard suppresses the call and the ordering assertion passes vacuously (proving nothing).
findings.location: Tests owned by WS3 section: MCP startup test, case (a)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test spec: 'case (a) creates a temporary tracker dir and patches `config.tracker_dir()` to return it, so the existing-dir guard passes and `run_ensures` is genuinely called.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit proving command for the WS3 test surface (e.g., `pytest tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py -v`) to at least one AC or a 'How to verify' subsection so done-ness is unambiguously demonstrable.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command (e.g., `pytest tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py -v`) is stated for any AC; each AC describes what the tests assert but leaves the verification invocation implicit, making it impossible to confirm done-ness from the plan text alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all six ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC6 or add a 'Verification' block: 'Run `pytest tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py -v` — all cases must be GREEN; run `rebar fsck` on a pending store and confirm the `ensures: N/M applied` line appears in stdout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the scope contradiction: either move the run_ensures timeout/attempts parameter addition into WS1's scope (and reference it as a WS1 deliverable in WS3's What section), or explicitly carve it out as an In-scope WS3 touch on WS1's artifact with a clear justification.
findings.finding: WS3's What section states 'WS3 adds an optional timeout/attempts passthrough to run_ensures' (modifying the run_ensures signature), but the Scope section explicitly lists 'the registry/run_ensures (WS1)' as Out-of-scope. An implementer reading the Scope section would conclude run_ensures is WS1's artifact and not to be touched in WS3, directly contradicting the What section's directive to modify it.
findings.location: What section ('WS3 adds an optional timeout/attempts passthrough') vs. Scope section ('Out: the registry/run_ensures (WS1)')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add a narrow carve-out: 'In: the timeout/attempts signature extension to run_ensures (required for MCP short-lock budget; all other run_ensures behavior remains WS1).' Remove it from the generic 'Out: the registry/run_ensures (WS1)' blanket exclusion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the no_mutate-never-sweep test ownership boundary: if WS3 provides basic no_mutate coverage, say so explicitly in the Scope In list and qualify WS5's ownership as 'the authoritative/extended suite'; if WS3 covers none, remove it from AC6.
findings.finding: The Scope section lists 'no_mutate-never-sweep behavioral suite (WS5)' as Out-of-scope, but AC6 simultaneously states that 'WS3's new test_fsck_ensures.py covers... no_mutate-never-sweep'. This creates an unresolved contradiction about which work stream owns and provides the no_mutate-never-sweep test coverage.
findings.location: Scope section ('Out: ... no_mutate-never-sweep behavioral suite (WS5)') vs. AC6 ('WS3's new tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py covers ... no_mutate-never-sweep')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5) that explicitly verifies the timeout/attempts passthrough: e.g. 'run_ensures is called with a non-None timeout; a test that patches write_lock to raise TimeoutError confirms run_ensures returns without raising and boot completes normally.'
findings.finding: The What section describes a new deliverable — the timeout/attempts passthrough parameter on run_ensures — but no Acceptance Criterion explicitly verifies that this parameter exists and functions correctly (e.g. that a short-budget call skips a contended lock rather than blocking). AC5 only verifies MCP call ordering and error resilience, not the lock-budget mechanism itself.
findings.location: What section ('WS3 adds an optional timeout/attempts passthrough to run_ensures') and AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out strategy for the fsck --repair behavioral change: either (a) document a --no-ensure flag to opt out of the sweep phase, (b) describe an expand-contract sequence (add sweep behind a flag first, then make it default), or (c) explicitly state why rollback is unnecessary and what the safe revert procedure is if the sweep causes issues.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges that 'existing fsck --repair callers now ALSO converge config — an acknowledged behavioral change' but provides no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequence. Under T4, acknowledgement alone is insufficient; a destructive/behavioral change requires an explicit reversibility mechanism.
findings.location: What section: 'This means existing fsck --repair callers now ALSO converge config — an acknowledged behavioral change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement plan or fixture (e.g., a timed lock-contention probe in CI) to derive/validate the MCP startup lock-timeout default rather than asserting 'a few seconds'.
findings.finding: The MCP startup lock timeout ('a few seconds') is an asserted heuristic with no measurement plan or derivation from observed lock-contention data.
findings.location: MCP server startup section — 'SHORT lock budget (a few seconds) so a contended lock skips rather than delaying boot'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the lowercase-tag-excluded-from-JSON convention (and the `^[A-Z_]+:` regex contract in `_transform_json`) in a CLAUDE.md section or inline doc-comment so future fsck report lines follow the same rule.
findings.finding: The lowercase-tag-excluded-from-JSON convention (relying on _transform_json's `^[A-Z_]+:` regex) is a novel implicit contract introduced by this plan with no ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the decision.
findings.location: 'Read-only ensures: N/M applied line' section — '_transform_json only lifts ^[A-Z_]+:-tagged structural issues'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a doc-comment above `_transform_json` and/or a CLAUDE.md entry explaining: 'Informational report lines use a lowercase tag (e.g., `ensures:`, `a3-remediation:`) and are intentionally excluded from JSON output by the `^[A-Z_]+:` filter; new structural issues must use an ALL_CAPS_UNDERSCORE tag to appear in JSON.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a try/except wrapper (log-and-continue) around the `ensures.run_ensures(tracker)` call in the `if do_repair:` block of `fsck_cli`, mirroring the explicit error-handling already required for the MCP startup path, so an unexpected sweep exception never aborts the fsck report.
findings.finding: The fsck --repair path says the ensure phase is 'log-and-continue' but never specifies a try/except wrapper around the `ensures.run_ensures(tracker)` call in `fsck_cli`. Per-unit failures are caught inside run_ensures, but an unexpected exception FROM run_ensures itself (e.g., corrupt lock file, unexpected I/O error) would propagate up and abort fsck before its report is printed — unlike the MCP path, which explicitly wraps the call in try/except.
findings.location: What section: 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair'; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair' section, add: 'Wrap the `ensures.run_ensures(tracker)` call in a bare try/except; on exception, log the error and append `ensures: sweep error — <exc>` to the report, then continue normally. This mirrors the MCP try/except and ensures the data-repair report is always printed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the output (or explicit suppression) of the `ensures: N/M applied` line when M=0 — either omit the line entirely when the registry is empty, or call out the expected text so tests and operators know what to expect.
findings.finding: The read-only `ensures: N/M applied` line is not specified to be suppressed (or have defined output) when M=0 (no ensures registered). Emitting `ensures: 0/0 applied — run rebar fsck --repair to converge` when there are no registered ensures is confusing noise; but the plan is silent on this case.
findings.location: What section: 'Read-only ensures: N/M applied line'; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'If M==0, suppress the ensures line entirely (no output). This avoids noise for stores that have not registered any ensures.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test case to `test_fsck_ensures.py` verifying that when `run_ensures` raises during `fsck --repair`, the command still completes successfully (data-repair results are reported, exit code is not changed by the sweep failure) — mirroring MCP startup case (c).
findings.finding: The fsck --repair sweep phase (a new user-facing flow) has happy-path and idempotency tests specified but NO test for the failure path where `run_ensures` raises or returns failed outcomes during the fsck sweep. The plan says this is 'log-and-continue' but no test in `test_fsck_ensures.py` is described that verifies fsck --repair continues (exits non-fatally, produces a report, exits cleanly) when the ensure sweep throws.
findings.location: Tests owned by WS3 / AC item 6 / test_fsck_ensures.py
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_fsck_ensures.py`, add a case that patches `ensures.run_ensures` to raise an exception, runs `fsck_cli(repair=True)`, and asserts the command completes without re-raising and that the report still contains the data-repair summary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test case (or extend an existing MCP startup test) that simulates a contended lock (patch `run_ensures` to simulate a lock-timeout/skip) and asserts MCP startup completes promptly and calls `build_server().run()` — verifying the 'short lock budget skips, never delays boot' guarantee.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough to `run_ensures` (used at MCP startup for 'a few seconds' lock budget so a contended lock skips), but no test exercises the contended-lock / timeout scenario to verify that MCP startup actually skips (does not hang or error-abort) when the lock is held. The three MCP tests cover ordering and an exception but not the timeout/skip-on-contention path specifically.
findings.location: MCP server startup section / AC item 5 / test_fsck_ensures.py MCP tests
findings.suggested_fix: Patch `run_ensures` to raise a lock-timeout exception (or return quickly without sweeping), assert `build_server().run()` is still called and `main()` returns without hanging. This covers the timeout passthrough wiring without needing a real lock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough on `run_ensures` is justified by ≥2 additional callers beyond MCP startup; if not, consider having MCP startup manage its own short-lock budget (e.g. a local `try: write_lock(..., timeout=3, attempts=1)` wrapper) rather than widening the WS1 `run_ensures` API signature for a single consumer.
findings.finding: The plan proposes adding optional `timeout`/`attempts` parameters to `run_ensures` (a WS1 public API) so MCP startup can pass a short lock budget. But at plan time only ONE call-site actually overrides these defaults (MCP startup with `timeout=<few seconds>, attempts=1`); `fsck --repair` calls `run_ensures` with default arguments. `write_lock` in `lock.py` (lines 300–313) already natively accepts `timeout` and `attempts`, so `run_ensures` would simply be forwarding them through. Parameterising the public API of a WS1 module for a single override consumer violates Rule-of-Three and YAGNI — the abstraction serves no current done-definition beyond MCP startup.
findings.location: What section — MCP server startup paragraph: 'WS3 adds an optional `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough to `run_ensures` that forwards to `write_lock`; default `None` keeps WS1's behavior unchanged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `rebar._store.ensures` (with `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) actually exists in the branch WS3 targets before execution; if WS1 is not yet merged, add an explicit prerequisite/dependency gate to the plan.
findings.finding: The plan's central premise 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`' is an empirically-checkable codebase assertion that is refuted by grep/read: `src/rebar/_store/` contains no `ensures.py`, and no source file anywhere defines or imports `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, or `EnsureOutcome`. Every surface WS3 wires (fsck --repair phase, _scan N/M line, MCP startup) imports from this non-existent module. If WS1 is truly not yet merged, WS3 cannot be executed as written.
findings.location: What section, first paragraph: 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites' section: 'WS3 requires WS1's branch to be merged to `origin/main` first. Verify `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py` exists and exports `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome` before starting WS3 implementation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite note confirming `rebar._store.ensures` (WS1) is merged and importable before WS3 work begins, or block the WS3 ticket on the WS1 ticket.
findings.finding: `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker)`, `registry_ids()`, `applied_ids(tracker)`, and `EnsureOutcome` does not exist in the codebase — `src/rebar/_store/` has no `ensures.py` and no grep hit for any of these symbols anywhere in `src/`. The plan treats these as already landed by WS1, but an implementer cannot proceed with WS3 until WS1 is complete.
findings.location: What section (opening paragraph): 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's top section: 'PREREQUISITE: WS1 (`rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) must be merged to `origin/main` before WS3 implementation starts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Locate or clarify the status of `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py` (the file is referenced in `fsck.py`'s docstring but is absent from the repo); confirm whether byte-parity for fsck report output is actually enforced anywhere, and update the plan accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan cites `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py` as the byte-parity golden test file that pins fsck report output format, stating 'no golden/byte-parity file needs editing'. However, this file does not exist: `tests/interfaces/` contains only subdirectories (`contracts/`, `facades/`, `io/`, `lifecycle/`, `queries/`, `store/`) and no top-level `.py` files. The `fsck.py` module docstring also cross-references this path, but the file is absent from the repo.
findings.location: Tests section: 'There is NO existing byte-parity golden for the fsck REPORT output — the only fsck goldens are… `tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py`'; also AC6: 'no existing fsck golden/byte-parity test changes'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the three unenumerated `fsck_cli` callers (`src/rebar/_lib_reads.py`, `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_compact_negative.py`, `tests/interfaces/store/test_tracker_config_e2e.py`) in the plan's scope/test-impact section and confirm each is unaffected by the additive `ensures: N/M applied` line.
findings.finding: The plan adds an `ensures: N/M applied` line to `_scan()` output, which is returned by `fsck_cli` to its callers. Two external callers outside `fsck.py`'s own dir are not enumerated: `src/rebar/_lib_reads.py:274` (calls `fsck_cli([], no_mutate=True)` and returns the text to library consumers) and two test files (`tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_compact_negative.py:94`, `tests/interfaces/store/test_tracker_config_e2e.py:113`). These callers receive the new line in fsck output but are not mentioned in the plan's scope or test-impact analysis.
findings.location: What section (Read-only `ensures: N/M applied` line) and Tests section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Tests section: 'The existing callers of `fsck_cli` outside its own module — `_lib_reads.py`, `test_compact_negative.py`, and `test_tracker_config_e2e.py` — do not assert on the `ensures:` line and require no changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `run_ensures()` must be called WITHIN `_repair_run()`'s reconciler-pause window (before `_reconciler_resume()`) or whether post-resume execution is intentionally acceptable, and add a sentence in the plan to make this ordering decision explicit.
findings.finding: The plan inserts `run_ensures(tracker)` AFTER `_repair_run()` returns, but `_repair_run()` re-enables the GHA reconciler in its `finally` block (line 658: `_reconciler_resume()`) before it returns. This means the ensure sweep runs with the reconciler already re-enabled, creating a window where a fresh reconciler pass can interleave with the ensure writes. The plan never specifies whether `run_ensures` should execute inside or outside the reconciler-pause window, leaving this ordering ambiguity unresolved.
findings.location: What section — 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair'; fsck.py lines 656-659 (_reconciler_resume in finally block)
findings.suggested_fix: If ensures should be part of the 'fully converged' state before the reconciler resumes, move the `run_ensures` call into `_repair_run` itself (before the `finally` block that calls `_reconciler_resume`). If post-resume is acceptable (ensures are idempotent and the reconciler cannot corrupt them), add an explicit acknowledgement in the plan: 'ensures are called after the reconciler resumes; this is safe because [reason]'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or add an explicit prerequisite gate) that `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, and `EnsureOutcome` is confirmed present in the codebase before WS3 work begins, since no such module currently exists in `src/rebar/_store/`.
findings.finding: The plan's opening 'What' section asserts 'WS1 landed rebar._store.ensures with run_ensures(tracker)->list[EnsureOutcome], registry_ids()->frozenset[str], and applied_ids(tracker)->set[str]' as a verified, already-landed API that WS3 can import. However, src/rebar/_store/ contains no ensures.py — the module and all three named callables are absent from the codebase. This is a T1(c) unverified (actually disproven) capability assertion: every WS3 integration point (fsck_cli import, _scan import, mcp_server import) depends on this module existing, yet it does not.
findings.location: What section, opening paragraph: 'WS1 landed rebar._store.ensures with run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]...'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard prerequisite line at the top of the plan: 'Prerequisite: WS1 must be merged and rebar._store.ensures verified present (confirms: run_ensures, registry_ids, applied_ids, EnsureOutcome) before any WS3 implementation begins.' Also add a CI gate (import check) in the WS3 test setup to catch missing dependency early.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief justification in the 'MCP server startup' subsection for why boot-time convergence is preferred over lazy/on-first-write alternatives (e.g., 'boot-time is chosen so the server always starts in a converged state, avoiding a write-lock acquisition mid-request'), or explicitly note that READONLY-gated servers should skip the sweep.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — calling run_ensures() at every MCP server boot in mcp_server.main() — without considering the established alternative of lazy convergence (e.g., run ensures on first write-tool invocation, or as a middleware wrapper). The plan justifies the choice only as 'best-effort converge the store' but does not explain why server-boot is preferable to lazy/on-demand execution, which would avoid the lock acquisition on every boot regardless of whether any write tool will be invoked.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP server startup' subsection; AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `.ensure-applied` is written atomically (temp-file + rename) and explicitly state that a corrupt/absent marker causes all units to re-sweep on next `--repair` run — confirming this is the designed, safe recovery path (T11: resumability + rollback).
findings.finding: The plan never specifies whether `.ensure-applied` is written atomically (e.g., via a temp-file-then-rename pattern) or in-place. A process killed mid-write leaves a corrupt/partial JSON file. The plan's `absent/garbage → set()` rule implies a re-sweep on the next `--repair` run, but this recovery guarantee is never stated, and the schema state of `.ensure-applied` after a partial failure is never asserted — violating T11(c) resumability and T11(e) rollback/back-out.
findings.location: What section — 'run_ensures acquires AND RELEASES the store write lock INTERNALLY (per-sweep)'; AC1; AC2 (idempotency check)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What section under 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair': 'The `.ensure-applied` marker MUST be written atomically via a temp-file + `os.replace()` rename so a mid-write process kill leaves no corrupt partial file; on next `--repair` a missing or corrupt marker is treated as `set()` (per WS1 spec) causing all ensures to re-sweep — this is the intended idempotent recovery path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit WS1→WS3 prerequisite gate: either confirm rebar._store.ensures (with run_ensures, registry_ids, applied_ids, EnsureOutcome) is shipped before WS3 begins, or define an interface contract/stub WS3 can validate against so the assumed API surface is verifiable before implementation starts.
findings.finding: rebar._store.ensures — the WS1-delivered module that ALL THREE WS3 surfaces depend on (run_ensures, registry_ids, applied_ids, EnsureOutcome) — does not exist anywhere in the codebase (src/rebar/_store/ has no ensures.py and none of its named exports appear in any file). The plan asserts 'WS1 landed rebar._store.ensures with run_ensures(tracker)->list[EnsureOutcome], registry_ids()->frozenset[str], and applied_ids(tracker)->set[str]' as a confirmed, already-delivered fact, but this is contradicted by the repo. If WS3 executes before WS1 ships, or if WS1's actual interface diverges from what WS3 assumes, no WS3 surface can be implemented.
findings.location: What section (opening paragraph); AC1, AC2, AC3, AC5 all depend on ensures.run_ensures/registry_ids/applied_ids
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to the plan: 'Prerequisites: WS1 must ship src/rebar/_store/ensures.py exporting run_ensures(tracker, *, timeout=None, attempts=None)->list[EnsureOutcome], registry_ids()->frozenset[str], applied_ids(tracker)->set[str], and EnsureOutcome with .status in {"ok","changed","failed"} before WS3 implementation begins.' Also add a smoke import assertion at the top of tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py so CI immediately surfaces a WS1 interface mismatch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `EnsureOutcome`'s exhaustive field names and status-value vocabulary (e.g., a `status: Literal['ok','changed','failed']` field) in this plan or by cross-reference to the WS1 spec, so the fsck report format (`C changed, F failed`) and the 'non-ok unit' logging rule can be implemented and tested without guessing.
findings.finding: `EnsureOutcome`'s exhaustive status-value vocabulary is referenced but never defined in this plan. The report format string `ensures: swept N unit(s); C changed, F failed` and the per-unit logging rule for 'any non-ok unit' both require an executor to know the complete set of outcome statuses (implied: `ok`, `changed`, `failed`) and which fields of `EnsureOutcome` carry them, but neither the plan nor any codebase file defines this enum (the module `rebar._store.ensures` does not exist in `src/rebar/_store/`).
findings.location: What section — 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair' paragraph; AC1 ('reports the swept units'); AC2 ('reports no ensure changes')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-section or table to the 'What' block: e.g. '`EnsureOutcome` is a dataclass/NamedTuple with fields: `unit_id: str`, `status: Literal["ok", "changed", "failed"]`, `detail: str`. C = count(o for o in outcomes if o.status=="changed"); F = count(o for o in outcomes if o.status=="failed").' If WS1 already defines this, add an explicit cross-reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the MCP startup fallback: specify whether 'missing store' is handled by a pre-call `if os.path.isdir(tracker):` guard (and thus not an except case) or by catching a named exception (and if so, name it), so the try/except clause can be written and tested unambiguously.
findings.finding: The MCP startup try/except fallback names three exception categories ('ImportError, a missing store, or any sweep error') but leaves 'a missing store' as an informal English phrase with no corresponding exception type. An implementer cannot write the except clause without knowing whether 'missing store' means `FileNotFoundError`, a custom `StoreNotFoundError`, a falsy `tracker_dir()` result guarded by `if not os.path.isdir(...)`, or something else.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP server startup' paragraph: 'Wrap the whole thing in a try/except that log-and-continues: an ImportError, a missing store, or any sweep error must never abort boot'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an ImportError, a missing store, or any sweep error' with: 'guard the call with `if os.path.isdir(tracker)` (handles missing-store without exception); then wrap the whole block in `except Exception` to catch ImportError and any sweep error — log at WARNING level and continue.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
